name: Hungry Earth #092
description: ATUONA Gallery of Moments - Hungry Earth. Underground poetry preserved on blockchain. Free collection - true to underground values. Decay

attributes.value:
Hiva Oa doesn't accept guests. It eats them.

You don't get this right away. First — just the wetness that wraps your skin like a second, unwanted body. Then — the smell. Not the romantic frangipani from guidebooks, but something ancient, hungry. Copra rotting in the sun. Earth digesting itself.

Kira stood on the airstrip — if you could call it that. A gash carved into the mountain where the G700 squeezed in like a knife between ribs.

"Kaoha nui," the pilot said, and it sounded like a warning.

Ule was already loading suitcases into the jeep. His movements had gotten slower, like the island was draining something vital from him. Battery drain — Kira thought. Like a smartphone in roaming, searching for signal and finding none.

The road to Atuona isn't a road. It's a scar on the island's body that refuses to heal. Black volcanic gravel crunches under wheels like bones. Mount Temetiu hangs above — 1,276 meters of pure hostility.

"Look," Ule pointed to the valley below. "Brel lived there. Before Gauguin."

Jacques Brel. Another European who came to die in paradise. His Beechcraft Bonanza still rusts somewhere in the jungle — metal carcass the island slowly digests.

Kira knew the statistics. Whites don't last long here. Gauguin — two years. Brel — three. Locals say the island chooses who to keep. Spits out the rest.

In the Jacques Brel Museum — four rooms of dust and disappointment — the caretaker slept right in his chair. His tattoos moved with each breath. Traditional Marquesan patterns, but mixed with something new. Kira looked closer: between ancient mana symbols, words broke through. "Bitcoin." "Ethereum." "HODL."

"He mines," Ule explained. "On solar panels. Says blockchain is the new mana. Distributed power."

Distributed power. Kira almost laughed. In 2019, crypto had already died and resurrected five times. And here, at the edge of the world, a Polynesian in a loincloth was building a mining rig between bone carving and copra drying.

Gauguin's house — "Maison du Jouir" — turned out to be a replica. The original rotted twenty years after his death. It wasn't even a lie, but something worse. An honest attempt to recreate what never was.

"He died up there," the guide pointed to the second floor. "May 8, 1903. Morphine overdose. Syphilis. Heart failure. Pick whatever works for Instagram."

The commodification of death. Kira thought about how Sotheby's sells "provenance" — a painting's ownership history. Previous owners: Paul Gauguin (died of syphilis), Ambroise Vollard (hit by car), mysterious Swiss collector (suicide). Each death adds value.

"What about the painting?" she asked. "Paradise?"

The guide shrugged. "Tourists always ask. There's no painting. Gauguin was broken, high on morphine. He painted his Contes Barbares — look at the faces. That's not paradise. That's hell."

Kira went out to the veranda. Below stretched the bay — the same one Gauguin painted over and over. Now some tech billionaire's yacht sat there. On board glowed the inscription: "Paradise.exe"

Ule smoked by the jeep. In his eyes was the same thing as in the locals'. Not fatigue — something deeper. Like the island had already started its work. Slow digestion. Pixel by pixel. Byte by byte.

"Let's go," he said. "Before it's too late."

But Kira knew — already too late. Hiva Oa doesn't let go. It takes its payment. Flesh or soul — without discrimination.

That evening in the hotel she opened her MacBook. Wi-Fi worked intermittently, like a dying man's pulse. On screen — email from the Vogue Russia editor: "Where's the piece? Deadline was yesterday."

Kira started typing: "Hiva Oa isn't an island. It's a stomach..."

Delete.

"On Hiva Oa time flows differently..."

Delete.

"I found paradise. It turned out to be a predator."

Send.

Send.
